hook.text: Rome moved 300 million gallons of water daily across hundreds of kilometers — by gravity alone.

buildup.text: Engineers used a slope of just 1 cm per 300 meters over entire mountain ranges.

discovery.text: Eleven aqueducts fed Rome's fountains, baths, and homes — the first water infrastructure.

twist.text: Some sections ran underground for kilometers to avoid enemy sabotage during wartime.

climax.text: Roman aqueducts worked for over 500 years. Some still carry water in Spain and France.

punchline.text: Gravity-powered engineering that outlasted the empire.

quiz.question: How did Roman aqueducts move water without pumps?
options: By using precise downward slopes driven by gravity
